title: Google pushes Gemini 3.5 Pro to July 17 and scraps the base architecture
subtitle: The delay lands amid a talent exodus to Anthropic and OpenAI, and roughly $225 billion has come off Alphabet's market cap since I/O — the cost of running a fresh pre-training cycle at frontier scale.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Google DeepMind pushed the general-availability launch of Gemini 3.5 Pro to July 17, 2026, and confirmed it has scrapped the existing Gemini 2.5 Pro architecture in favor of a ground-up rebuild. The company unveiled 3.5 Pro at Google I/O on May 19 and told developers to expect general availability the following month. It did not arrive. Enterprise testers reported gaps in coding performance, token efficiency, mathematical reasoning, SVG scene generation, image quality, and long-horizon multi-step reasoning that Google decided could not be closed with fine-tuning.

Restarting pre-training at frontier scale costs in the low hundreds of millions of dollars and consumes months of GPU time. Google chose it anyway, which reads two ways: either the prior candidate fell short of what Alphabet leadership was willing to ship as a flagship, or the bar the competitive field set on the same date — Fable 5, GPT-5.6, Claude Opus 4.8 — moved above what the old architecture could reach. The rebuilt model is targeting a 2 million token context window, a Deep Think reasoning layer, and autonomous workflow capabilities.

The delay lands on top of a brutal week of researcher departures. Noam Shazeer, a Gemini co-lead, left for OpenAI. Nobel laureate John Jumper, plus Jonas Adler and Alexander Pritzel, moved to Anthropic. Markets took roughly $225 billion off Alphabet's market cap. Google is still the only lab with an in-house TPU stack, a hyperscale cloud, and a mature consumer distribution surface, but the sequence — missed target, top-team attrition, scrapped architecture — is the sharpest challenge to its frontier position since the original ChatGPT release.

For learners: the frontier is not a lead you keep by shipping on schedule; it is one you keep by knowing when not to ship. Google chose the harder path of a rebuild rather than the easier path of a mediocre GA. That trade only makes sense if the leadership is confident the next release wins, and if the team can hold together long enough to finish it. Watch July 17 closely — it is a rare public test of whether a scrapped-and-rebuilt frontier model can catch up in weeks rather than years.

sources.title: Google Delays Gemini 3.5 Pro Launch to July 17 for Full Architectural Rebuild — BigGo Finance
